claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 7 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child I'm asking about. Their name is Alex, and they're 7 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 7 years old.

Now I need to ask: **Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! And one more important question: **Does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I understand. Since Alex doesn't live with you, I need to ask: **Do you make regular financial contributions towards Alex's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or accommodation), and if so, how much do you contribute?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute regularly towards Alex's upkeep. I help with things like clothes, food, and other necessities. I contribute £50 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Just to clarify: **How long has Alex been living away from you - is it less than 8 weeks, or has it been longer than that?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been longer than that - about 3 months now. Alex is in local authority care.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that Alex is in local authority care and has been for about 3 months.

This is an important question: **Does Alex spend any regular time at home with you each week? For example, does Alex spend at least 24 hours a week at home, or stay at least 2 nights in a row each week with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't come home to stay with me. They're at the care home full-time.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me just confirm one final detail:

**Is the local council paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance costs while in care?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm not sure about that - I don't think they've mentioned anything to me about that. I just know Alex is in the care home and I'm still contributing what I can towards their needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Based on the information you've provided, I can now make an assessment of your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

When a child is in local authority care, the local council typically does pay towards their accommodation and maintenance. According to the rules, if your child goes into care for more than 8 weeks, your Child Benefit payments are usually affected. After 8 weeks, you're generally no longer entitled to Child Benefit unless certain exceptions apply.

The main exceptions for children in care are:
- If the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home with you, or
- If the child spends 2 nights in a row each week or 7 days or more in a row at home

Since Alex has been in local authority care for about 3 months (which is longer than 8 weeks) and doesn't come home to stay with you regularly, you would not qualify for Child Benefit for Alex under the current rules, even though you are making financial contributions towards their upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Eligibility Decision: Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

I commend you for continuing to contribute to Alex's needs, but based on the circumstances, you would not qualify for Child Benefit. If Alex's situation changes in the future - for example, if they start spending at least 24 hours a week at home with you, or staying 2 consecutive nights each week - you may become eligible and should contact HMRC at that time to discuss your situation.

reasonings: Alex is 7 years old and in local authority care for approximately 3 months (over 8 weeks). While the claimant lives in the UK and contributes £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, the child does not live with them and does not spend regular time at home (no 24 hours per week visits, no 2 consecutive nights per week, or 7+ consecutive days). According to the rules, when a child goes into care for more than 8 weeks, Child Benefit usually stops unless exceptions apply - such as the child spending at least 24 hours a week at home. Since Alex is in full-time care with no regular home visits, the claimant does not meet the exceptions required to continue receiving Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child (Alex, age 7) who has been in local authority care for approximately 3 months. While the claimant lives in the UK and makes regular financial contributions, they are not eligible for Child Benefit because Alex has been in care for longer than 8 weeks and does not spend sufficient regular time at home to meet the exceptions to the 8-week rule.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 7 years old and in local authority care for approximately 3 months (over 8 weeks). While the claimant lives in the UK and contributes £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, the child does not live with them and does not spend regular time at home (no 24 hours per week visits, no 2 consecutive nights per week, or 7+ consecutive days). According to the rules, when a child goes into care for more than 8 weeks, Child Benefit usually stops unless exceptions apply - such as the child spending at least 24 hours a week at home. Since Alex is in full-time care with no regular home visits, the claimant does not meet the exceptions required to continue receiving Child Benefit.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child (Alex, age 7) who has been in local authority care for approximately 3 months. While the claimant lives in the UK and makes regular financial contributions, they are not eligible for Child Benefit because Alex has been in care for longer than 8 weeks and does not spend sufficient regular time at home to meet the exceptions to the 8-week rule.
